does: This paper introduces CAPER, a method for improving text-to-SQL systems through clause-level supervision. Rather than relying only on query-level execution correctness or token-level dense labels, CAPER automatically generates intermediate supervision signals by applying counterfactual interventions on SQL abstract syntax trees. These clause-aligned labels identify which semantic decisions in the SQL query caused success or failure. The authors train CAPER-9B, a lightweight clause process reward model, that provides fine-grained feedback at SQL clause boundaries for both policy optimization and candidate verification. On BIRD and Spider benchmarks, this approach achieves up to 15.3% relative improvement in execution accuracy over GPT-3.5 and demonstrates strong root-cause error localization with 84.53% accuracy and 90.60% MRR on held-out failure cases.
connects: This work builds on the broader line of text-to-SQL research that has moved beyond simple end-to-end query generation toward more interpretable and debuggable systems. It responds to limitations in existing supervision approaches: query-level signals provide insufficient guidance about intermediate errors, while token-level dense supervision misaligns with semantic SQL decisions and struggles with execution-equivalent query variants. The paper connects to recent advances in process reward modeling and intermediate step supervision in language models, adapting these techniques from broader reasoning tasks to the specific structure of SQL. It also engages with longstanding challenges in automatically annotating training data at scale, proposing counterfactual intervention on syntax trees as a scalable alternative to manual clause-level labeling.
enables: This work opens several directions for more interpretable and trustworthy SQL generation systems. The clause-aligned supervision framework could extend beyond text-to-SQL to other structured code generation tasks where intermediate correctness matters. The failure-localization capability suggests applications in interactive debugging systems where models explain which parts of generated queries caused failures, potentially enabling more efficient human-in-the-loop refinement. The lightweight PRM design demonstrates that intermediate supervision need not require massive additional models, suggesting similar approaches for other resource-constrained settings. Future work could investigate whether clause-level feedback improves sample efficiency during training, how the method scales to more complex multi-schema scenarios, and whether learned clause-level reasoning patterns transfer across different database schemas and domains.
